Part (d): Which quality control processes are to be used by the data analysis centre to assess the data quality and to handle problems that are observed relating to data and data analysis?
Part (e): Describe the general principles to be observed in the preparation of the Case-Report Form and the organization of the form.

Part (a): The survival times (in months) of 12 patients who have undergone a treatment are given below: $$10, 25^+, 20, 15^+, 60, 18^+, 16, 17, 28^+, 2, 30^+, 35$$ where $+$ denotes right censored observations. Assuming that the survival times follow exponential distribution with mean $1/\lambda$, obtain the maximum likelihood estimate for the mean. Obtain the maximum likelihood estimate for $S(t)$. Compute $S(t)$ at $t=10$ months.

Part (b): The following data represents the survival times of extremely ill AIDS patients observed in days: $$2, 72, 51, 60, 33, 27, 14, 24, 21$$ Compute the Kaplan-Meier estimate of the survival function $S(t)$. Sketch the graph of $S(t)$ against $t$. Compute $S(10)$ and $S(25)$, and compare.
Part (c): Explain briefly the key categories of clinical trials data that are to be collected.
Part (d): Explain Phase I and Phase II clinical trials. What are MTD and DLT related to Phase I trials? Explain the traditional design approach used in Phase I trials.
Part (a): Explain the basic differences among the chance and special causes of variation that affect process results.
Part (b): State your agreement or disagreement to the statement given below, with brief justification: "A statistically controlled process will always produce 100% results within tolerance limits."
Part (c): Derive the control limits for the control chart for defects in a constant and varying number of sample units.
Part (d): Explain the chart which describes the sensitivity of the sampling plan to detect the lot quality level for acceptance.
Part (e): What is Cusum chart? Explain the methodology of using it for process control.

Part (a): Evaluate the ARL to detect a shift in the process average (no change in process variation) by one unit of standard deviation in the higher side. Assume use of $\bar{X}$-chart with subgroup size 5 and probability of not detecting the shift is at most 0.05.
Part (b): Explain the exponentially weighted moving average chart with at least one example of its potential application.
Part (c): Describe the variable sampling scheme and compare it with the attribute sampling scheme.

Part (d): An improvement in the process has resulted in an increase of $C_{pk}$ from 0.6 to 0.95. Estimate the reduction in % of nonconforming products. Assume the following: Process average has not changed, $C_p = C_{pk}$ and process is in statistical control.

Part (a): In the ISS examination of a certain year, the distribution of scores in Statistics Papers I, II, III and IV is found to follow a 4-variate normal distribution with known parameters. Indicate how you would estimate the percentage of candidates in that examination faring better (i) in Papers I and II compared to Papers III and IV, both in the aggregate, and (ii) in Paper I compared to Paper IV.
Part (b): In multivariate analysis, often the issue of handling too many variables poses a big problem. How would you propose to solve the problem? Indicate the procedure.

Part (c): Assuming the form of the conditional distribution of a subset $(X_1, X_2, \dots, X_q)$ of random variables $(X_1, X_2, \dots, X_p)$, $q < p$, following multivariate normal law for fixed values of $(X_{q+1}, \dots, X_p)$, derive the expression for the conditional mean of $X_1$ when $X_2 = x_2, \dots, X_p = x_p$.

Part (d): Define Fisher's linear discriminant function. Give a classifier rule for classifying an individual based on his or her measurements on $p$ identified variables, into one of two mutually exclusive and exhaustive classes, clearly stating the underlying assumptions and explaining the notation you have used.
Part (e): Show that in random sampling from a $p$-variate normal distribution, the sample variances and covariances follow Wishart distribution. Identify the parameters.
Part (a): Show that if $X_{p \times 1}$ follows $N_p(\mu, \Sigma)$ then $(X-\mu)'\Sigma^{-1}(X-\mu)$ follows central chi-square distribution with $p$ degrees of freedom.

Part (b): (i) Suppose $X_{p \times 1}$ follows $N_p(\mu, \Sigma)$ with $E(X_i) = \mu$ for all $i$, $Var(X_i) = \sigma^2$ for all $i$ and $Cov(X_i, X_j) = \rho \sigma^2$ for $i \ne j$. Show that $$\frac{1}{\sigma^2(1-\rho)} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^p (X_i - \mu_i)^2 - \frac{\rho}{1+(p-1)\rho} \left(\sum_{i=1}^p (X_i - \mu_i)\right)^2 \right]$$ follows chi-square distribution with $p$ degrees of freedom, where $\mu_i = \mu$ for all $i$. (ii) Define multiple correlation coefficient $R_{1.23\dots p}$ of $X_1$ on $X_2, X_3, \dots, X_p$ taken together, based on the observed set of data $x_{ij}$, $i=1(1)p$, $j=1(1)n$, and write down its expression. Derive the simplified form of $1 - R^2_{1.23\dots p}$ for the special case where $r_{1i} = r$ and $r_{ij} = \rho$ for all $i, j \ge 2, i \ne j$.
Part (c): Derive the maximum likelihood estimators for the parameters of a multivariate normal distribution.
Part (d): Discuss the procedure for testing the null hypothesis $H_0: \mu^{(1)} = \mu^{(2)}$ regarding the mean vectors of two multivariate normal distributions having identical dispersion matrix.

Part (a): Discuss the usefulness of the principle of randomization in the context of design of experiments. Use the following set of random numbers to obtain the layout of a randomized block design with 5 blocks of size 4 each for testing the treatments A, B, C and D: $$ \begin{array}{llllllllllll} 518 & 275 & 950 & 305 & 490 & 333 & 068 & 088 & 389 & 933 & 548 & 275 \\ 950 & 305 & 490 & 334 & 069 & 736 & 545 & 774 & 864 & 062 & 951 & 005 \end{array} $$

Part (b): A $2^5$ experiment has been laid out in 4 replicates, in each replicate confounding one of the 3-factor interactions. Give the compositions of the 4 key blocks, and write down the ANOVA table.
Part (c): What is a Latin Square Design? What are the main disadvantages of using such a design in field experiments? Indicate the procedure for obtaining the layout of a 5x5 Latin Square Design.

Part (d): A $3^2$ experiment with factors A and B each at levels 0, 1 and 2 is to be designed in $2r$ replicates of 3 incomplete blocks each, such that in $r$ replicates $AB$ is confounded, while in the remaining $r$, $AB^2$ is confounded, the other factorial effects remaining unconfounded in the entire design. Give the layout of the 3 incomplete blocks of each of the two types of replicates, and write down the ANOVA table.

Part (e): The factor A has $p$ levels which require relatively large experimental units. Another factor B has $q$ levels requiring relatively small experimental units. Suggest a suitable design for accommodating both the factors in a single experiment. Discuss the procedure for testing the main effects A and B, as also the interaction effect AB.

Part (a): It is desired to use a randomized block design with 4 blocks of size 6 each for testing the effects of 5 treatments A, B, C, D and E. In each block, treatments B, C, D and E are replicated once each, while treatment A is replicated twice to ensure more precise estimation and testing for A. Using a suitable model, give the expressions for different sums of squares, and write down the ANOVA table. Discuss the procedures for testing equality of all treatment effects, as also for equality of effects of A and B, if the hypothesis of equality of all treatment effects is rejected.

Part (b): A $3^3$ factorial experiment with factors A, B and C each at levels 0, 1 and 2 is to be laid out in 9 incomplete blocks of size 3 each, totally sacrificing information on the factorial effects $ABC$, $AB^2C$ and $AB C^2$. Give the layout of the design. If this basic pattern is replicated $r$ times, discuss the procedure for analyzing the experiment.

Part (c): Discuss, in detail, the procedure for analysis of covariance in a completely randomized design for testing the effects of $t$ treatments with $r_1, r_2, \dots, r_t$ replications respectively in the presence of just one concomitant variable.

Part (d): A $2^4$ experiment is proposed to be carried out in 2 incomplete blocks of size 8 each per replicate, retaining full information on all main effects, and sacrificing equal amount of information on the remaining four factorial effects each. Suggest a scheme of partial confounding ensuring the above, and give the intrablock subgroup in each replicate. Discuss the method of analysis for the data.

Part (a): Write a C-program that will generate every $n$-th integer, beginning with `nstart` (i.e., $i = \text{nstart}, \text{nstart} + n, \text{nstart} + 2n$, etc.), ending with `nstop`. Calculate the sum of those integers that are evenly divisible by some positive integer $k$.
Part (b): Write a C-program to find whether a given matrix is a symmetric matrix and print the comment.

Part (c): Write a C-program to calculate the value of the series $x - \frac{x^3}{3!} + \frac{x^5}{5!} - \dots + (-1)^n \frac{x^{2n+1}}{(2n+1)!}$ by defining a recursive function and printing the result.
Part (d): Write an R-program to fit a linear relationship of $y$ on $x$, to get the summary of the relationship and to predict $y$ for a given value of $x$.
Part (e): Write an R-program to calculate the median of a set of observations.

Part (a): Write a C-program that will examine each character in a line of text and determine how many of the characters are letters, how many are digits, how many are white space characters, and how many are other kinds of characters (e.g. punctuation characters) and print them.
Part (b): Write a C-program to create a linear linked list, delete an existing component from the list defining suitable functions.
Part (c): Write a C-program to evaluate $y = x^n$, where $x$ and $y$ are floating-point values and $n$ can be an integer or floating point and print it.
Part (d): Write a C-program to fit a normal distribution to a given observed frequency distribution and test for its goodness of fit and print the result.
Part (a): Solve the following two-person zero-sum game using graphical procedure: $$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & -3 \\ 3 & 5 \\ -1 & 6 \\ 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 2 \\ -5 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

Part (b): If the demand for a certain product has a rectangular distribution between 4000 and 5000, then find the optimal order quantity if the storage cost is \( \text₹1 \) per unit and the shortage cost is \( \text₹2 \) per unit.

Part (c): A supermarket has two salesgirls at the counter. If the service time for each customer is exponential with mean 4 minutes and if people arrive in a Poisson fashion at 10 per hour, then find the expected percentage of idle time for each salesgirl.

Part (d): Find out ML estimators of the parameters in the density \( f(x) = \frac{1}{b} e^{-x/b}, x > 0, b > 0 \) based on the following censored scheme: [Text indicates missing data regarding specific scheme parameters; transcription follows raw text] where n and r are respectively the sample size and number of censored observations.
Part (e): Defining a location-scale family, outline the procedure for obtaining the coefficient of BLUEs.

Part (a): A computer contains 10000 resistors. When any resistor fails, it is replaced. The cost of replacing a resistor individually is \( \text₹1 \) only. If all the resistors are replaced at the same time, the cost per resistor comes down to 35 paise. The percent of surviving, \( S(t) \), at the end of \( t \)-th month is given in the table below. Derive the optimum replacement plan.
Part (b): A random sample of 25 observations drawn from Weibull model (with shape parameter 2 and scale parameter = 4) is given in the table below. Obtain the moment estimates of the parameters.
Part (a): Discuss the sources of demographic data in India and also point out the uses and limitations of these data.
Part (b): Explain the stationary and stable models. Discuss the population situation when stationary and stable populations are identical.
Part (c): Explain briefly the uses of life table. In usual notation, prove that \( d_x = -l_x \cdot \frac{d}{dx} \ln(l_x) \).
Part (d): Define crude death rate, specific death rate and standardized death rate. Interpret these rates.
Part (e): State the general procedure and steps for the construction of life tables.
Part (a): Explain an abridged life table and discuss its different columns. Discuss the King's method for its construction.
Part (b): What do you mean by fertility? Define crude birthrate, general fertility rate, specific fertility rate and age-specific fertility rate. How are these rates computed in practice?
Part (a): Define hazard function and survival function. Obtain the same for an exponential distribution.

Part (b): If \( f(t) \), \( F(t) \) and \( h(t) \) are the density, distribution and hazard functions of a random variable \( T \), then show that \( h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{1-F(t)} \). Also establish a suitable relationship between \( h(t) \) and reliability function.
Part (c): If \( p_x \) is the probability that a person aged x will survive to age x+1, then show that \( p_x + nq_x = 1 \), where \( _np_x \) [context implies notation].
Part (d): Discuss some specific situations in which it would be difficult or inefficient to perform clinical trials.
Part (e): What are the principles for ethical clinical trials?

Part (a): Define Type-I and Type-II censoring schemes. Obtain the maximum likelihood estimator of parameter \( \theta \) in exponential distribution under the above censoring schemes. Also obtain Fisher information for the parameter.
Part (b): Discuss various phases involved in a clinical trial. Also discuss the pros and cons of each phase.
Part (a): Distinguish between process control and product control. Discuss the situations where they are used.
Part (b): Discuss various sources of assignable causes and random causes of variations. Also state how they are detected in a manufacturing process.
Part (c): Define the terms ASN, AOQ, ATI and ARL for an acceptance sampling plan.
Part (d): What is an acceptance sampling plan? Discuss a single sampling plan \( (n, c) \), where the sampling is carried out using a binomial model. Find \( P_a \), if n=10, c=3 and p=0.05.
Part (e): State the importance of exponentially weighted moving average charts. How are these used in practical situations?
Part (a): Discuss the importance of control charts for variables.
Part (b): Obtain the control limits for \( (\bar{X}, R) \) and \( (\bar{X}, S) \) charts when standards are known and unknown.
Part (c): In usual notation, show that \( c_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2}{n-1}} \frac{\Gamma(n/2)}{\Gamma((n-1)/2)} \), where \( c_2 \) is a constant used for constructing control limits.

Part (d): Discuss in detail the double sampling plan \( (N, n_1, c_1, n_2, c_2) \) stating the assumptions followed in both the stages. Hence or otherwise, obtain the ASN function of this sampling procedure.
Part (a): Define p-variate normal distribution and obtain its characteristic function. Assume mean vector \( \mu \) and dispersion matrix \( \Sigma \).
Part (b): Define Hotelling's \( T^2 \) and mention its application. Show that \( T^2(p, m) = \frac{mp}{m-p+1} F(p, m-p+1) \).
Part (c): Explain the importance of principal components and discuss the method for extraction of principal components.
Part (d): Define Wishart distribution and obtain its characteristic function.
Part (e): Define canonical variates and canonical correlation. Give your interpretation.

Part (a): State the chief properties of Wishart distribution. If \( A_i \sim W_p(\Sigma, n_i), i=1, 2, \dots, k \), then show that \( A = \sum_{i=1}^k A_i \) has the Wishart distribution with parameters \( \Sigma \) and \( n \), where \( n = \sum_{i=1}^k n_i \), assuming the independence of \( A_i \)'s.
Part (b): Define multiple correlation coefficient and obtain its non-null distribution.
Part (a): What are the basic principles of design of experiments? Explain them and their roles.
Part (b): Define completely randomized design, and mention its merits and demerits. Also give its statistical analysis.
Part (c): Define Latin square design with its applications, advantages and disadvantages.
Part (d): Explain analysis of covariance with its applications. Give an illustration for the use of analysis of covariance in identifying the response variable (y) and concomitant variable (x).
Part (e): Explain split-plot design and give some situations where split-plot design can be suitably adopted.

Part (a): What are factorial experiments? The table provided gives the layout and the results of a \( 2^3 \)-factorial design laid out in four replicates to determine the effect of Nitrogen (N), Potash (K) and Phosphate (P) on potato crop yield. Analyze the design and draw conclusions.
Part (b): What is confounding? A \( 2^3 \)-factorial experiment is conducted in 2 blocks of size 4 each, in 3 replicates. Identify the confounded effect in each replicate.
Part (c): Define a Randomized Block Design (RBD) specifying the statistical model associated with the design. Carry out a complete analysis of RBD with one missing observation.
Part (a): Given a five-digit positive integer, write a C program to find the sum of individual digits.
Part (b): Write a C program to pick up the largest tender from a set of tenders assuming that Tender-id and Tender-value for each tender are specified.
Part (c): Express \( (A-B)*C \) in infix and postfix forms.
Part (d): Write a program in R to plot Q-Q plot assuming that the two samples (\( X_i, i=1, 2, \dots, 25 \) and \( Y_i, i=1, 2, \dots, 250 \)) are drawn from normal population.
Part (e): Given n positive integers, write R code to find their ranks.

Part (a): Illustrate call by value and call by reference, and advantages as well as disadvantages of each method. Write a C function to evaluate the series without using a built-in function: \( \sin(x) = x - \frac{x^3}{3!} + \frac{x^5}{5!} - \dots \)
Part (b): Given \( X \sim N(\mu, \sigma) \), where \( \mu \) and \( \sigma \) are specified, write a C program to compute \( P(X) = \int_{-\infty}^x \text{Density of } N(\mu, \sigma) dt \).

Part (a): Compare Simple Random Sampling Without Replacement (SRSWOR) and Simple Random Sampling With Replacement (SRSWR) and find the value of $n$ such that the variance of the sample mean in SRSWOR is exactly half of the variance of the sample mean in SRSWR of the same size.

Part (b): Consider the simple linear regression model: $$y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$ where $\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2, \ldots, \varepsilon_n$ are independent and identically distributed with mean zero and constant variance $\sigma^2$. Show that the least squares estimates $\hat{\beta}_0$ and $\hat{\beta}_1$ are linear functions of $y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n$. Also compute the variance-covariance matrix of $(\hat{\beta}_0, \hat{\beta}_1)$ and its determinant.

Part (c): Let $Y_t = Z_t + \theta Z_{t-1}$, where $\{Z_t\}$ is a sequence of i.i.d. random variables with mean zero and variance $\sigma_Z^2$. Show that the real-valued function defined on $\mathbb{Z}$ as: $$\gamma(h) = \begin{cases} 1 & h = 0 \\ \rho & h = \pm 1 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ is an autocovariance function if and only if $|\rho| \leq \dfrac{1}{2}$.

Part (a): Find the spectral density function $f(t)$ of a continuous parameter stationary process having autocorrelation function $\rho(t) = e^{-t^2}$, $-\infty < t < \infty$. Also find the value of $f\!\left(\sqrt{\log 16}\right)$.

Part (b): From a bivariate population of $N$ units, a simple random sample $(x_i, y_i)$, $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$, is drawn without replacement with corresponding sample means $(\bar{x}_n, \bar{y}_n)$. Show that: $$\text{Cov}(\bar{x}_n, \bar{y}_n) = \left(\frac{1}{n} - \frac{1}{N}\right)S_{xy}$$ where $S_{xy} = \dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^N (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})$ is the population covariance.

Part (c): Explain the term exponential smoothing. When is exponential smoothing most useful? Interpret the smoothing constant $\alpha$. What is the range of $\alpha$? How is $\alpha$ related to the degree of smoothing?

Part (a): A simple random sample of $n$ clusters, each containing $M$ elements, is drawn from $N$ clusters in the population. Show that the sample mean per element $\bar{y}$ is an unbiased estimate of the population mean $\bar{Y}$ with variance: $$V(\bar{y}) = \frac{1-f}{nM}\,S^2\left[1 + (M-1)\rho\right]$$ where $f = n/N$ is the sampling fraction and $\rho$ is the intracluster correlation coefficient.

Part (b): Using the first-order approximation to the variance of the ratio estimator $R_n = \bar{y}_n / \bar{x}_n$, show that: $$\frac{|\text{Bias in } R_n|}{\sqrt{\text{Var}(R_n)}} \leq \frac{\sqrt{V(\bar{x}_n)}}{\bar{x}_N}$$ where $\bar{x}_N$ is the population mean of $x$.

Part (c): A village has five orchards, containing 15, 30, 25, 10, and 20 trees respectively. The yields (in 10 kg) of these five orchards are 16, 35, 29, 12, and 24 respectively. Selecting a sample of two units at the 2nd and 4th positions, estimate the total production of five orchards along with standard error using the Horvitz-Thompson estimator.

Part (a): Let $(X, Y)$ have the joint pdf: $$f(x, y) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{x} & |y| < x,\; 0 < x < 1 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ Show that the regression of $Y$ on $X$ is linear but the regression of $X$ on $Y$ is not linear.

Part (b): Given the following moment matrices: $$X'X = \begin{pmatrix} 10 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 10 \end{pmatrix}, \quad X'Y = \begin{pmatrix} 10 & 20 \\ 20 & 10 \\ 30 & 20 \end{pmatrix}$$ estimate the structural equation model: $$y_{1t} = \beta_{12}\, y_{2t} + \gamma_{11}\, x_{1t} + \gamma_{12}\, x_{2t} + u_{1t}$$ $$y_{2t} = \beta_{21}\, y_{1t} + \gamma_{23}\, x_{3t} + u_{2t}$$ using the Two-Stage Least Squares (2SLS) method, where $y_1, y_2$ are endogenous and $x_1, x_2, x_3$ are predetermined.
Part (c): State the methods of detecting the presence of heteroscedasticity in a regression model. Discuss any one of them in detail.

Part (a): Define Laspeyres' index number and Paasche's index number. If $L(p)$ and $P(p)$ respectively represent Laspeyres' and Paasche's index numbers for price, and $L(q)$ and $P(q)$ represent Laspeyres' and Paasche's index numbers for quantity, then show that: $$\frac{L(p)}{L(q)} = \frac{P(p)}{P(q)}$$

Part (b): Define autoregression of order $k$. Consider the autoregression process: $$U_t = a\xi + \varepsilon_t, \quad -\infty < t < \infty$$ where $\varepsilon_t, \varepsilon_{t+1}, \ldots$ and $\xi$ are independent variables with zero mean and unit variance. Show that the process is stationary with autocorrelation: $$\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \cdots = \frac{a^2}{1 + a^2}$$
Part (c): Explain the time series model and its components. Briefly discuss how each component is measured and their economic/statistical significance.
Part (a): Explain the Stratified Random Sampling method and the problems associated with stratification. Also write down the advantages of Stratified Random Sampling over Simple Random Sampling.
Part (b): Explain the double sampling plan for attributes and derive the expression of the Operating Characteristic (OC) curve in the double sampling plan.
Part (c): What is multicollinearity? Discuss the problem of multicollinearity using a three-variate linear regression model and its consequences on OLS estimation.

Part (a): For a Markov process: $$u_t = \rho\, u_{t-1} + v_t, \quad |\rho| < 1$$ where the random variables $v_t$ are such that $E(v_t) = 0$, $\text{Var}(v_t) = \sigma_v^2$ and $\text{Cov}(v_t, v_s) = 0$ for $t \neq s$. Show that: (i) $\displaystyle u_t = \sum_{r=0}^{\infty} \rho^r v_{t-r}$ (ii) $\text{Var}(u_t) = \dfrac{\sigma_v^2}{1 - \rho^2}$ (iii) $\text{Cov}(u_t,\, u_{t-s}) = \dfrac{\rho^s}{1 - \rho^2}\, \sigma_v^2$

Part (b): Discuss the Durbin-Watson test for autocorrelation. The data in the following table are the OLS residuals of a consumption function: $$\hat{C}_t = -3{\cdot}02 + 0{\cdot}93\,Y_t$$ Calculate the Durbin-Watson $d$-statistic. Write your conclusion. (Table for $d$-statistic significance points is attached.)

Part (c): State the conditions of identification for the structural form of a system of simultaneous equations. Discuss the identification of the following model, assuming $Y$'s are endogenous and $X$'s are predetermined variables: $$Y_1 = \alpha_{10} + \alpha_{12}Y_2 + \alpha_{13}Y_3 + \beta_{11}X_1 + u_1$$ $$Y_2 = \alpha_{20} + \alpha_{23}Y_3 + \beta_{21}X_1 + \beta_{22}X_2 + u_2$$ $$Y_3 = \alpha_{30} + \alpha_{31}Y_1 + \beta_{31}X_1 + \beta_{32}X_2 + u_3$$ $$Y_4 = \alpha_{40} + \alpha_{41}Y_1 + \alpha_{42}Y_2 + \beta_{43}X_3 + u_4$$

Part (a): Explain AR($p$), MA($q$), ARMA($p$, $q$) and ARIMA($p$, $d$, $q$) processes. How would you find out the appropriate values of $p$, $d$ and $q$ while modelling a given time series? State the procedure to estimate the parameters of the ARIMA model.
Part (b): Define the Dickey-Fuller (DF) test. How would you use it to find out if the given time series contains a unit root? If a unit root exists, how would you characterise such a time series?

Part (c): Explain autocovariance and autocorrelation functions. If $y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n$ are $n$ observations made at $n$ successive time points of a stationary process, then in the usual notation define the autocovariance matrix and autocorrelation matrix of order $n$. For $n = 3$, show that: $$-1 \leq \frac{\rho_2 - \rho_1^2}{1 - \rho_1^2} \leq 1$$
